original_abstract: Traditional animal models and static cell cultures remain the standard for investigating mercury ion (Hg(II)) transport, yet they frequently fail to replicate the complex human intestinal microenvironment or provide real-time physiological monitoring. Consequently, the precise mechanisms governing Hg(II) absorption in the human gut remain poorly understood. In this study, we developed a gut-on-a-chip platform integrated with transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) and electrochemical sensors to simulate the formation of the intestinal barrier and monitor Hg(II) transport in situ. By applying fluid shear stress (0.02 dyne/cm²) and cyclic mechanical strain (1%, 0.15 Hz), we successfully recreated a dynamic cellular microenvironment. Using this biomimetic system, we simultaneously tracked electrochemical signatures of Hg(II) absorption and TEER-measured physical damage across varying mercury concentrations. Our findings reveal that increasing tensile strain from 1% to 5% led to a 23.59% rise in Hg(II) absorption, a phenomenon correlated with the upregulated expression of the mechanosensitive ion channel Piezo1 and the metal transporter DMT1. This integrated platform offers a robust tool for studying heavy metal toxicity and the mechanobiology of intestinal transport.
humanized_abstract: Mercury ion (Hg2+) transport mechanisms in human gut have not yet been fully elucidated. Current studies on Hg2+ transport generally rely on conventional animal experiments and static cell culture models, which do not well replicate the in vivo intestinal microenvironment. This study presents a gut-on-a-chip platform with integrated transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) and electrochemical sensors. It was applied to investigate the Hg2+ transport within the developed gut barrier model under the stimulation of fluid shear stress (0.02 dyne/cm²) and cyclic mechanical strain (1%, 0.15 Hz). An increase in the tensile strain from 1% to 5% was shown to increase the uptake of Hg2+ by 23.59% and this increased uptake was found to be consistent with physical damage to Caco-2 cell barrier measured by TEER, as well as an enhanced expression of mechanosensitive ion channel Piezo1 and the metal transporter DMT1. The developed gut-on-a-chip platform will greatly facilitate the research on heavy metal toxicity and the mechanobiology of intestinal transport.
